title: Partial hydrogenation of unsaturated polyethers: A convenient route to curable terpolymers for lithium batteries
abstract:
Partial Hydrogenation of Unsaturated Polyethers:
A Convenient Route to Curable Terpolymers
for Lithium Batteries
F. ALLOIN, J.-Y. SANCHEZ
LEPMI, Laboratoire d’Electrochimie et de Physicochimie des Mate´riaux et des Interfaces, UMR no. 5631 CNRS-INPG,
(associe´ a` l’UJF), B.P.75, 38402 Saint Martin d’He`res Ce´dex, France
Received 31 March 2000; accepted 22 May 2000
Published online xxx
ABSTRACT:
Catalytic hydrogenation of unsaturated polyethers has been investigated.
The partial reduction of the alkenyl functions appears a convenient way to prepare
terpolymers from dipolymers. The free-radical crosslinking of the partially hydroge-
nated polyethers provides amorphous networks at room temperature. Ionic conductiv-
ity of the polymer electrolytes based on these networks is markedly increased, as
compared to the nonhydrogenated ones. The electrochemical stability window of these
electrolytes is retained. In addition, this process seems easy to scale up. The resulting
terpolymers provide networks whose mechanical properties are appropriate for lithium
polymer batteries. © 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Inc. J Polym Sci A: Polym Chem 38: 2900–2909,
2000
Keywords:
polymer electrolytes; network; lithium salt; terpolymer; hydrogenation
INTRODUCTION
Solid polymeric electrolytes are under intensive
investigation because of their potential applica-
tions in electrochemical devices, such as batteries
or electrochromic windows. In particular, the
safety due to the absence of volatile components
makes them suitable for large lithium batteries.
Owing to (i) the solvating ability of its ethylene
oxide repeat unit, (ii) its electrochemical stability
versus metallic lithium, and (iii) its commercial
availability, high molecular weight poly(oxyethyl-
ene) POE has been widely used as a host polymer.
However, due to the crystallinity of the polymer
matrix whose melting point is close to 65 °C, these
polymer electrolytes are semicrystalline at sub-
ambient and ambient temperatures. This results
in poor conductive materials, as it is well known
that crystallinity is detrimental to ionic conduc-
tivity. In addition, due to a dramatic decrease in
Young modulus upon melting, the most conduc-
tive POE electrolytes exhibit a creeping tendency
from 50 to 60 °C.
In order to decrease, or to remove, the polymer
electrolyte crystallinity, while keeping a high con-
ductivity level, several new host polymers have
been proposed.1,2
Our contributions deal with several amorphous
polymer electrolytes, meeting the speciﬁcations of
lithium batteries, that is, long-term electrochem-
ical stability in order to obtain a high cyclability
of the battery, mechanical properties consistent
with thin laminated cells and a thermal stability
allowing one to minimize the emission of volatile
components in the event of a short circuit. We
have, in particular, opted for unsaturated poly-
(oxyethylene), prepared by Ring Opening Poly-
merization—ROP—of ethylene oxide and allyl
glycidyl ether.3 Their double bonds enable the
polymer electrolyte to be crosslinked, providing
Correspondence to: J.-Y. Sanchez
Journal of Polymer Science: Part A: Polymer Chemistry, Vol. 38, 2900–2909 (2000)
© 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Inc.
2900
 good mechanical properties4 at the operating tem-
perature of the batteries (40–80 °C) and up to 120
°C. Our selection of a two-step process—ROP fol-
lowed by free-radical crosslinking—rather than a
one-step one has been dictated by redox stability
concerns. Indeed polyurethane-ether or polyether-
methacrylate networks, obtained respectively by
polycondensation of oligomeric polyethylenegly-
cols PEGs or by free-radical copolymerization of
mono and dimethacrylate macromonomers, con-
sist of low molecular weight unreacted products.
The latters such as, respectively, diols (PEGs) and
isocyanates, or macromonomers, which easily dif-
fuse from the bulk to the electrode, are thermo-
dynamically unstable versus metallic lithium and
enforces therefore a membrane washing before
use. If the soluble material extraction is easy and
necessary at the academic level to value the in-
trinsic properties of the network, this extra step is
not suitable at the industrial scale. In our case,
the crosslinking deals with medium to rather
high molecular weight polyethers and the lowest
chain lengths of the remaining soluble polymers
are still much higher than the entanglement
threshold, that is, the critical molar mass. Lastly,
the unreacted vinylic or allylic groups of our un-
saturated polycondensates are by far less reactive
versus metallic lithium than methacrylate, alco-
hols, or isocyanates.
The copolymerizations, performed in a high
pressure reaction vessel, were initiated either by
Vandenberg type catalyst or by potassium tert-
butoxide. Both initiators provide a high conver-
sion rate, the former resulting in broader molec-
ular weight distributions. ROP offers several op-
portunities, such as the incorporation of double
bonds or (and) ionic functions. These copolymer-
izations provide solvating ionomers with a single
cation mobility5,6 well-adapted to an industrial
production. Among the copolymers, dipolymers
have been prepared from mixtures of ethylene
oxide (EO) and allyl glycidyl ether (AGE); both
oxiranes being industrial monomers. In such a
dipolymer, EO units mainly provide the solvating
ability,
while
the
AGE
units
allow
further
crosslinking of the polyether to be performed.
Network electrolytes stemming from anionic ini-
tiation copolymers exhibit a wide electrochemical
stability window3 and among the best ionic con-
ductivities known, for true polymer electrolytes,
that is, without addition of external plasticizers
or solvents.
Besides its crosslinking function, AGE disrupts
the long-range regularity of poly(oxyethylene), so
decreasing the crystallinity. A conductivity in-
crease at subambient temperatures, necessitates
obtaining fully amorphous crosslinked electro-
lytes at those temperatures. A convenient way to
increase the ambient and subambient conductiv-
ities, while keeping the solvating ability provided
by the OE repeating unit, would consist in an
increase in the content of the AGE units. Indeed
the resulting copolymers become amorphous or
markedly less crystalline. But at the same time,
the crosslinking density increases in the net-
works, decreasing the chain segmental motion
and therefore the ionic conductivity. In order to
overcome this drawback we may perform ring-
opening terpolymerization in order to keep the
same content of defects as in the previous OE/
AGE, while decreasing the percent of AGE and
then the crosslink density. Although terpolymers
such as ABS or EPDM are well known and man-
ufactured, this way may present some drawbacks
for polymer electrolytes. Indeed the remaining
crystallinity as well as the crosslinking efﬁciency
is strongly dependent on the distribution, con-
trolled by six reactivity ratios, of the third mono-
mer units in the polymer chain. Furthermore the
third monomer to be selected must be electro-
chemically stable and must not affect the solvat-
ing ability of the polymer chain. We may therefore
expect, by addition of a third monomer, an in-
crease of the copolymer cost. In order to solve
these problems, we have selected to prepare a
terpolymer from an unsaturated dipolymer by a
simple chemical modiﬁcation, consisting in a par-
tial hydrogenation of the double bonds.7
EXPERIMENTAL
Synthesis
Coordinated Polymerization
Ethylene oxide (Air gaz), and triethylaluminium
are used as received. Allyl glycidyl ether is previ-
ously distilled over calcium hydride, in argon,
before storage in a glove box under argon. The
polymerizations are performed in a high pressure
reactor vessel Parr. Monomers and initiator are
introduced, in the previous glove box, in two
stainless steel cylinders end-capped and are
pushed under argon in the reactor. The polymer-
izations are performed in toluene at 100 °C in
argon, under a 7 bar pressure. After 24 h, the
active centers are deactivated by the addition of
water. Toluene is then evaporated and the copoly-
HYDROGENATION OF UNSATURATED POLYETHERS

Ethylene oxide (Air gaz), and triethylaluminium
are used as received. Allyl glycidyl ether is previ-
ously distilled over calcium hydride, in argon,
before storage in a glove box under argon. The
polymerizations are performed in a high pressure
reactor vessel Parr. Monomers and initiator are
introduced, in the previous glove box, in two
stainless steel cylinders end-capped and are
pushed under argon in the reactor. The polymer-
izations are performed in toluene at 100 °C in
argon, under a 7 bar pressure. After 24 h, the
active centers are deactivated by the addition of
water. Toluene is then evaporated and the copoly-
HYDROGENATION OF UNSATURATED POLYETHERS
mer, abbreviated as Van, dissolved in methanol
and precipitated in diethyl oxide. After ﬁltration
and washing with diethyl oxide, the polymer is
thoroughly dried under vacuum.8,9
Hydrogenation has been performed in a stainless
steel high pressure reactor vessel, using Raney
Nickel as a catalyst. The Raney nickel is obtained
by leaching a commercial Ni-Al alloy (50–50 wt
%) with sodium hydroxide solution (6 mol z l21) at
boiling temperature following a method described
elsewhere.10 The latter is kept in an aqueous so-
dium hydroxide solution (1 mol z l21). It is washed
three times with water and methanol just before
Hydrogenation is carried out in methanolic so-
lution at constant hydrogen pressure and temper-
ature with a stirring speed of 1600 rpm. Before
the beginning of the hydrogenation, the reactor
containing a suspension of the catalyst in a meth-
anolic solution of Van (10g in 150 mL) is bled by a
hydrogen ﬂow. The reaction progress may be es-
timated by the pressure evolution in the reserve.
At the end of the reaction, most of the catalyst is
removed with a magnet, the complete puriﬁcation
being achieved by ﬁltration. After the methanol
evaporation, the copolymer is dried in vacuum
and then stored in a glove box, in argon.
The copolymer crosslinking is achieved by free-
radical polymerization, using benzoyl peroxide as
initiator. The initiator is dissolved in an acetoni-
trile solution of the copolymer. According to the
typical casting procedure, the acetonitrile solu-
tion is cast in a glass ring on a PTFE plate. After
the acetonitrile evaporation, the samples are
heated at 70 °C for several hours. The membranes
are washed several times in methanol to remove
the soluble material. We have previously checked
that the Soxhlet extraction provides about the
same percent of soluble materials as the metha-
nol washing. After the removal of methanol, poly-
mer electrolyte samples are obtained by swelling
the ﬁlm in an acetonitrile solution of the salt. The
salt composition is obtained by the difference in
weight between the dried membranes before and
A Netzsch STA409 thermal analyzer has been
used to measure the characteristic temperatures
Tg, Tm. Samples are ﬁrst cooled from room tem-
perature to 2100 °C, then heated at 10 °C z min21
to 150 °C. After this ﬁrst heating cycle, the sam-
ples are quenched to 2100 °C and then heated
again at 10 °C.min21 up to 150 °C. All the tem-
perature values Tm, Tg were determined in this
1H NMR spectra have been recorded in CD3CN,
Stress-strain curves have been drawn, using a
Minimat from Rheometrics. Samples of about 200
mm thickness, 5-mm wide and 5-mm length can
then be tested in a temperature range from room
temperature to 200 °C in a controlled atmosphere.
The test was performed at 1 mm/min at 40 °C in
air. We use a 20 N strength sensor.
Conductivity has been determined, under dy-
namic vacuum, by a.c impedance spectroscopy, in
the frequency range 5 Hz to 13 MHz, using a
HP4192A impedance analyzer. The samples are
sandwiched between two stainless steel elec-
trodes, while the complete impedance cell is as-
sembled and stored in an argon dry glove-box.
Impedance is measured at various temperatures,
after an equilibrium time of 1 h at each temper-
The electrochemical stability has been checked
by cyclic voltammetry, using microelectrodes. We
have selected a metallic microdisc of platinum (f
5 25 mm) as a working microelectrode. The vol-
tammograms have been recorded at 80 °C, using a
The chemical modiﬁcation of natural rubber and
other polydienes by partial hydrogenation is well-
known and may be performed at an industrial
scale. The reaction has been performed, using
Raney Nickel as a catalyst, on a 95/5 OE/AGE
copolymer. This molar ratio, determined by 1H
NMR, does not mean a statistical distribution of
the AGE units. This is evidenced by the three
melting points: 214, 11, and 40 °C, which corre-
spond to POE average sequence lengths ranging
between 8 and 27 oxyethylene units, while a per-
fect distribution should correspond to 19 OE se-
quences between two AGE units.
1H NMR Characterization Principle
The content of hydrogenated double bonds has
been determined by 1H NMR. Different hydrogen
pressure and catalyst/double bond ratios have
been used. The NMR spectrum, in CD3CN, of the
partially hydrogenated Van shows six peaks
whose chemical shifts and assignments are listed
in Table I and indicated according to the following
The relative area of peaks c and e, allows the
hydrogenation efﬁciency to be calculated with a
Several experiments have been performed, at
room temperature, under a 10 bar hydrogen pres-
sure, varying the reaction time but keeping the
same concentration in double bonds, 7.1022 mol z
l21 and the same amount (231 mg) of catalyst.
Table II shows that the degree of hydrogenation,
close to 50%, is quite independent of the reaction
time. In order to overcome this limitation we have
varied the other hydrogenation parameters. Thus
we have ﬁrst investigated the temperature inﬂu-
ence and then the catalyst amount (Table II). The
degree of hydrogenation is only slightly improved
(56% against 48%), when performing the reaction
at a much higher temperature, 80 °C as compared
to 20 °C and even when increasing markedly the
reaction time. On the other hand, an increase by
a factor of three of the catalyst amount increases
notably the degree of hydrogenation, and has pro-
vided a copolymer, abbreviated as VanH75, which
degree of hydrogenation is 75%. The resulting
VanH75, consisting of one unsaturated unit for 76
oxyethylene units should provide crosslinked
properties and high ionic conductivities. In order
to appraise the ability of the Raney Nickel hydro-
genation to provide a quantitative degree of hy-
drogenation, we have submitted the same unsat-
urated copolymer to very drastic experimental
A reaction temperature close to 80 °C.
A 2.2 grs amount of catalyst for 1.2.1022 mole
of double bonds (7.1022 mol.l21).
A 90% hydrogenation yield has been obtained
but the rate to increase the conversion from 75 to
90% is very low. That could probably be related to
a decrease, with the reaction progress, of the
probability for a double bond to be adsorbed on
the catalyst surface. Indeed, with the hydrogena-
tion progress, the content in unsaturated bonds
decreases while the increasing hindrance of the
catalyst surface by the saturated macromolecular
chains leads to a kind of catalyst poisoning.
Despite the difﬁculties in reaching a quantita-
tive hydrogenation, this catalytic way is very at-
tractive. In addition we must remember that a
75% degree of hydrogenation is enough to provide
Inﬂuence of Temperature, Time, and
Catalyst Amount on Degree of Hydrogenation
1H NMR Spectrum: Chemical Shifts and
HYDROGENATION OF UNSATURATED POLYETHERS
a suitable crosslink density, providing both good
mechanical properties and high conducting poly-
Afterwards we will use the following abbrevia-
VanHx copolymer Van hydrogenated at x %.
The thermal analyses show that hydrogenation
does not modify Tg and Tm of the linear copoly-
mers (Table III ). This result might be expected as
the POE block length and the distribution of the
other moieties (AGE) in the polyether are not
modiﬁed by the reaction. On the contrary hydro-
genation changes the network melting point (Ta-
ble III ). This is in agreement with previous re-
ports in which we evidenced that the crosslinks
reduce the crystallinity and melting point of un-
saturated polyethers.3 Now, hydrogenation of al-
lylic moieties affects the crosslink density and
should therefore modify network crystallinity and
melting point. Thus NVanH75% exhibits the
highest crystallinity degree, close to 30%, and the
highest melting point. The latter remain never-
theless lower than that observed for linear copoly-
mers. Tg’s are roughly similar for all the samples,
the variation, in this crosslink density range, hav-
ing a weak inﬂuence on Tg value. Electrolytes
based on NVan, NVanH50, and NVanH75 are
amorphous, at room temperature ('25 °C), in the
whole salt concentration range investigated. Sur-
prisingly NVan/LiTFSI Tg’s remain low, even for
rather high salt concentration, O/Li 5 8, and
seems less affected by salt dissolution than poly-
condensates11 or anionic synthesized OE/AGE co-
In fact, the Tg increase, upon salt dissolution,
does not affect similarly electrolytes based on
NVan, NVanH50, and NVanH75 as shown by the
comparative plots 1/Tg versus salt concentration
(Fig. 1), which slopes are respectively 1.8.1024,
2.3.1024, and 2.2.1024. From these data, it seems
that salt/polymer interactions are stronger when
using hydrogenated NVan as host polymer. This
might be due to the modiﬁcation of glycigyl ether
moiety, 75% or 50% of the allylic ethers being
replaced by propylic ones, slightly more basic. But
Characteristic Temperatures and
Crystallinity Rate of the Different Van Copolymers
1/Tg versus salt concentration (mol z l21). Glass-transition temperature
dependence on salt concentration for raw and hydrogenated copolymers.
the content of these dangling groups only repre-
sents a molar fraction of 4% in the terpolymer.
One might also assume that these modiﬁed dan-
gling ethers make easier transient crosslinks
through interchain cation solvation.
To investigate the degree of crosslinking we per-
formed, on the nonhydrogenated network, an
usual swelling study based on Flory equations.12
The latter, performed in methanol allows the av-
erage molecular weight between two links to be
determined. Using an interaction parameter X1,2
5 0.113 at 25 °C, an average molecular weight
close to 800g/mol has been found. This value is
close to that inferred from NMR spectra on the
copolymer that is, the average poly(oxyethylene)
sequences between two AGE units. These results
are also in good agreement with the assumption
of an almost total reaction of the allylic double
The crosslinked membranes were washed several
times in methanol to eliminate polymer soluble
chains. Table IV summarizes the results. Due to
the double bond decrease, perhaps emphasized by
a heterogeneous distribution of the dangling al-
lyloxy double bonds, the amount of soluble mate-
rial increases in hydrogenated networks. Replace-
ment of Van copolymers by anionic synthesized
OE/AGE copolymers3 whose AGE distribution is
homogeneous, should improve the crosslinking ef-
The stress-strain test has been performed on a
crosslinked sample of 200 mm thickness, at 40 °C
and has shown an elongation at break higher
than 1000% for NVanH75, as compared to 200%
for NVan in the same experimental conditions.
Hydrogenation reduces, as expected, the Young
modulus by factor 10, as hydrogenated copoly-
mers have a lower crosslink density. These prop-
erties are well in accord with the thin battery
concept and should enable the lowering of the
electrolyte thickness, thus decreasing its ohmic
The ionic behavior of polymer electrolytes has
been investigated with lithium triﬂuoromethane
sulfonyl imide Li(CF3SO2)2NOLiTFSIO, from
washed membrane. Indeed we have established
that this salt improves the ionic conductivity of
polymer electrolytes. More recently ab initio cal-
culations performed on the anion15 and on the
lithium ion pair16 have shown on the one hand
that the negative charge is well delocalized, on
the other that the anion should be stable versus
The best conductivity of NVan/LiTFSI electro-
lytes has been obtained for a rather high salt
concentration, namely O/Li 5 8. The comparative
Arrhenius conductivity plots (Figs. 2 and 3) show
that conductivities close to 7.1026 S z cm21, and
3.1025 S z cm21 at 25 °C are, respectively, reached
From medium to highly concentrated electrolytes
that is, O/Li # 16, the complexes are fully amor-
phous (Table V). Moreover, even for less concen-
trated electrolytes, that is, O/Li . 16, the melting
temperature is close to 20 °C, leading therefore to
amorphous polymer electrolytes at room temper-
versus polymer electrolyte composition shows, in
a wide salt concentration range, the conductivity
gain provided by hydrogenation (Fig. 4). Figure 5
compares the conductivity maxima obtained for
the different networks, NVan, NVanH50, and
NVanH75 results in a conductivity gain ranging
between 4 and 10 in the whole temperature
range. Thus NVanH75 electrolyte reaches 1 mil-
lisiemens at # 70 °C, while NVanH50 one exhibits
conductivities close to those obtained with NVan/
LiTFSI. Such a gain is a deciding asset as it
allows the ohmic drop in the electrolyte to be
decreased by a factor 4 to 10 at the battery oper-
ating temperature, as compared to most of the
polymer electrolytes. A high degree of hydrogena-
tion is therefore required to observe a marked
increase in conductivity, which cannot be ex-
plained by Tg’s which, surprisingly, are higher in
Percentage of Insoluble Material for the
HYDROGENATION OF UNSATURATED POLYETHERS
the case of NVanH75 electrolytes (see part Ther-
mal analysis). Now the conductivity of polymer
electrolytes depends on the concentration of
charge carriers and their mobility, in relation to
the segmental mobility. So, in order to discrimi-
nate between these two parameters, it is some-
times proposed to compare the conductivities ver-
sus salt concentration with respect to the same
reduced temperature: T 2 Tg. It is therefore as-
sumed that the conductivities are compared at
the same segmental mobility, thus allowing the
solvating/dissociating ability of different host
polymers to be compared. Conductivity compari-
son at T 5 Tg 1 80 °C, Figure 6, shows a deﬁnite
gap between NVanH75 and NVan. The parallel
increase of conductivities and Tg’s of the hydroge-
nated electrolytes with respect to the nonhydro-
genated ones was unexpected. The fact that the
conductivity is increased when the segmental mo-
tion, according to the Tg values, is supposed to
Conductivity dependence versus T21 for NVanH50/LiTFSI electrolytes.
Conductivity dependence versus T21 for NVanH75/LiTFSI electrolytes.
decrease might be attributed to a contribution to
ion transport by the fast side-chain motions. Such
assumption, recently reported by Watanabe et
al.17 about comb polyethers with a dangling di-
(oxyethylene) moiety, or by Ikeda et al.18 is in
agreement with the solvating ability of propoxy
groups. As the polymer electrolytes are com-
pletely amorphous above room temperature we
may ﬁt the conductivity data with a free-volume
The values of B range between 0.04 and 0.09 eV,
as in most of our previous crosslinked polymer
electrolytes11 while the ideal transition tempera-
ture To is close to the Tg values.
Cyclic voltammetries have been performed at 80
°C on a sample NVanH75/LiTFSI using platinum
microelectrode. Platinum allows one to check the
electrolyte stability up to 4.1 volts versus Li/Li1,
but undergoes with lithium to the formation of
alloys Li3Pt2 and intermetallic compounds LiPt2.
We may observe (Fig. 7) that, after the reduction
scan, the reverse scan shows three peaks. We
attribute these peaks respectively at 0, 0.6, and
1.4 V versus lithium to oxidation of pure metallic
lithium plated on the surface and oxidation of
LixPt. The polymer electrolyte is stable, at least
up to 3.9 volts versus Li/Li1 and thus appears
compatible with several intercalation materials.
In fact, hydrogenation does not modify the elec-
trochemical stability of the polymer electrolytes.
Furthermore, even if unreacted double bonds still
Inﬂuence of Salt Concentration on Tg for Hydrogenated and Nonhydrogenated NVan
Isothermal representation of network conductivities versus salt concentra-
HYDROGENATION OF UNSATURATED POLYETHERS
remain after crosslinking, they do not induce
modiﬁcations on electrochemical stability of the
The partial double bond hydrogenation of unsat-
urated polyether seems a convenient and elegant
way to produce well-deﬁned unsaturated terpoly-
mers. Satisfactory degree of hydrogenation has
been obtained allowing a 75% conversion to be
reached. This process is well adapted to an indus-
trial scale-up, partial hydrogenations enabling a
variety of amorphous poly(oxyethylene) to be tai-
lored, without restricting the electrochemical sta-
bility window of the polymer electrolytes. More-
over a neat improvement of conductivity, in the
whole temperature range, has been observed by
increasing the degree of hydrogenation and these
polymer electrolytes range between the more con-
ductive one, along with our previous crosslinked
polyethers3,11 and branched comb-shaped poly-
ethers.17 In addition, these networks obtained
Comparison of best conductivity levels for different networks.
Comparison of conductivity at constant reduced temperature.
from high molecular weight polyethers, combine
chain entanglements and chemical crosslinks,
which enable their shaping into very thin electro-
lytes. As the ohmic resistance of the electrolyte is
proportional to the ratio thickness/conductivity,
the combination of thin ﬁlm and high conductivity
should minimize the ohmic drop in the battery.
